stem: The nurse is caring for a 68-year-old male client with stage 4 chronic kidney disease who missed his last two dialysis appointments. After reviewing the electronic health record, the nurse recognizes the client is most likely experiencing a specific complication. What actions should the nurse take, and what parameters should the nurse monitor to assess for improvement?

itemContext.sbar: Situation: This is the nurse for John Smith in room 402, calling at 10:30. He is reporting new-onset muscle weakness and palpitations. Background: Mr. Smith is a 68-year-old male with stage 4 CKD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. He was admitted for uremic symptoms and has missed his last two scheduled hemodialysis sessions. Assessment: His ECG shows prominent peaked T waves. He is lethargic. A stat lab draw shows a serum potassium of 6.8 mEq/L. Recommendation: I need orders for immediate intervention for hyperkalemia.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the nurse for John Smith in room 402, calling at 10:30. He is reporting new-onset muscle weakness and palpitations.
Background: Mr. Smith is a 68-year-old male with stage 4 CKD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. He was admitted for uremic symptoms and has missed his last two scheduled hemodialysis sessions.
Assessment: His ECG shows prominent peaked T waves. He is lethargic. A stat lab draw shows a serum potassium of 6.8 mEq/L.
Recommendation: I need orders for immediate intervention for hyperkalemia.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 10:25
Temperature: 37.0°C (98.6°F)
Heart Rate: 58/min, irregular
Blood Pressure: 150/92 mmHg
Respiratory Rate: 18/min
SpO2: 97% on room air

tabs.title: Laboratory Results
tabs.content:
STAT Basic Metabolic Panel (10:20):
  • Sodium: 136 mEq/L
  • Potassium: 6.8 mEq/L (High)
  • Chloride: 101 mEq/L
  • Bicarbonate (HCO3): 18 mEq/L (Low)
  • BUN: 85 mg/dL (High)
  • Creatinine: 5.2 mg/dL (High)
  • Glucose: 110 mg/dL
  • eGFR: 20 mL/min/1.73 m² (Low)

tabs.title: Nurse's Note
tabs.content: 10:15: Client reports feeling 'very weak' and that his 'heart is fluttering.' Appears lethargic but is oriented to person, place, and time. Neuromuscular exam reveals 3/5 strength in all extremities. Cardiac auscultation reveals an irregular rhythm. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema noted.

tabs.title: Medication Administration Record
tabs.content:
Scheduled Home Medications:
  • Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
  • Metformin 500 mg PO BID
  • Sevelamer 800 mg PO with meals

potentialCondition.rationale: The client's missed dialysis, elevated serum potassium of 6.8 mEq/L, muscle weakness, and ECG with peaked T-waves are classic signs of severe hyperkalemia, a life-threatening complication of chronic kidney disease.

actionsToTake.rationale: Correct. Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane to protect against life-threatening arrhythmias caused by hyperkalemia. It is the first-line emergent treatment to prevent cardiac arrest.

actionsToTake.text: Administer regular insulin and dextrose IV
actionsToTake.rationale: Correct. Insulin activates the Na-K-ATPase pump, which shifts potassium from the extracellular fluid into the cells, effectively lowering the serum potassium level. Dextrose is given concurrently to prevent hypoglycemia.
Incorrect. This is a potassium-sparing diuretic. Administering it would dangerously increase the already critical serum potassium level and is absolutely contraindicated.
Incorrect. This intervention does not directly lower serum potassium and can lead to fluid volume overload in a client with stage 4 CKD, potentially worsening their condition.
Incorrect. Administering potassium to a client with severe hyperkalemia is a lethal error and is absolutely contraindicated as it would cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias.

parametersToMonitor.rationale: Correct. Hyperkalemia causes characteristic ECG changes (peaked T waves, widened QRS). Continuous monitoring is essential to assess for resolution of these changes and prevent cardiac arrest.
Correct. This is the definitive laboratory value to confirm the effectiveness of interventions aimed at lowering extracellular potassium. It should be rechecked after treatment.
Incorrect. This parameter assesses the kidney's concentrating ability, which is not a relevant or priority indicator in the acute management of life-threatening hyperkalemia.
Incorrect. While vital signs are always monitored, blood pressure is not the specific parameter used to evaluate the immediate effectiveness of hyperkalemia treatment.
Incorrect. While electrolyte balance is important, the immediate life-threatening concern is hyperkalemia, making the serum potassium level the priority for monitoring.
